theorem_statement:
Let \(K \subset \mathbb C\) be compact, let \(U \subset \mathbb C\) be an open set containing \(K\), and let \(f : U \to \mathbb C\) be holomorphic. Let \(A\) be a subset of the extended plane \(\widehat{\mathbb C} = \mathbb C \cup {\infty}\) such that \(A\) contains at least one point from every connected component of \(\widehat{\mathbb C} \setminus K\). Then for every \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists a rational function \(r\), all of whose poles lie in \(A\), such that

\[
\sup_{z \in K} |f(z)-r(z)| < \varepsilon.
\]

In particular, if \(\mathbb C \setminus K\) is connected, then \(A\) may be chosen to be \({\infty}\), and \(f\) can be uniformly approximated on \(K\) by polynomials.
raw_proof_text:
We prove the theorem in four steps.

First, we reduce \(f\) to a finite sum of simple rational kernels. Since \(K\) is compact and \(U\) is an open neighborhood of \(K\), we may choose a finite union of small closed squares \(Q\) such that

\[
K \subset \operatorname{int}(Q) \subset Q \subset U.
\]

Let \(\Gamma\) be the positively oriented boundary of this finite union, with internal edges cancelled. Then \(\Gamma\) is a finite union of piecewise smooth closed curves contained in \(U \setminus K\). By the Cauchy integral formula applied to this finite contour system, for every \(z \in K\),

\[
f(z) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\Gamma} \frac{f(\zeta)}{\zeta-z},d\zeta.
\]

The integrand \((\zeta,z) \mapsto f(\zeta)/(\zeta-z)\) is continuous on the compact set \(\Gamma \times K\), because \(\Gamma\cap K=\varnothing\). Therefore the contour integral is uniformly approximable on \(K\) by Riemann sums. Thus, for any \(\delta>0\), there are points \(\zeta_1,\ldots,\zeta_N \in \Gamma\) and complex coefficients \(c_1,\ldots,c_N\) such that the rational function

\[
R_0(z) = \sum_{j=1}^N \frac{c_j}{\zeta_j-z}
\]

satisfies

\[
\sup_{z\in K}|f(z)-R_0(z)|<\delta.
\]

So it remains to show that each simple pole \(\zeta_j\), which lies in \(\widehat{\mathbb C}\setminus K\), can be moved to the chosen point of \(A\) in the same component of \(\widehat{\mathbb C}\setminus K\), while changing the function only slightly on \(K\).

Second, we prove the local pole-moving lemma. Let \(a,b\in \mathbb C\setminus K\). Suppose that

\[
|a-b| < \operatorname{dist}(b,K).
\]

Then for every \(z\in K\),

\[
\left|\frac{a-b}{z-b}\right| < 1.
\]

Hence the geometric series gives

\[
\frac{1}{z-a}
=

\frac{1}{z-b-(a-b)}

\frac{1}{z-b}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac{a-b}{z-b}}

\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(a-b)^n}{(z-b)^{n+1}},
\]

uniformly for \(z\in K\). Therefore \(1/(z-a)\) can be uniformly approximated on \(K\) by rational functions whose only pole is at \(b\), namely the finite partial sums

\[
\sum_{n=0}^{M} \frac{(a-b)^n}{(z-b)^{n+1}}.
\]

Thus, if two poles are sufficiently close in \(\mathbb C\setminus K\), then a simple pole at one can be replaced by rational functions with a pole only at the other.

Third, we move poles along paths. Fix one of the poles \(\zeta_j\). Let \(C\) be the connected component of \(\widehat{\mathbb C}\setminus K\) containing \(\zeta_j\), and choose \(a_C\in A\cap C\). If \(a_C\neq \infty\), choose a path \(\gamma\) in \(C\) from \(\zeta_j\) to \(a_C\). Since \(\gamma([0,1])\) is compact and disjoint from \(K\), its distance from \(K\) is positive. Partition \(\gamma\) into finitely many sufficiently short subsegments

\[
p_0=\zeta_j,\ p_1,\ldots,p_m=a_C
\]

so that

\[
|p_{\ell+1}-p_{\ell}| < \operatorname{dist}(p_{\ell+1},K)
\]

for every \(\ell\). Applying the local pole-moving lemma repeatedly, we can uniformly approximate \(1/(z-p_\ell)\) on \(K\) by rational functions with pole only at \(p_{\ell+1}\). Iterating, we approximate \(1/(z-\zeta_j)\) uniformly on \(K\) by rational functions with pole only at \(a_C\).

If \(a_C=\infty\), then \(C\) is the unbounded component. First move the finite pole \(\zeta_j\) along a path in the unbounded component to a point \(b\) with \(|b|\) very large compared with \(\sup_{z\in K}|z|\). Then for \(z\in K\),

\[
\frac{1}{z-b}
=

-\frac{1}{b}\cdot \frac{1}{1-\frac{z}{b}}

-\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^n}{b^{n+1}},
\]

uniformly on \(K\). Truncating this Taylor series gives a polynomial approximation. Thus a pole in the unbounded component can be moved to \(\infty\), meaning it can be eliminated in favor of a polynomial term.

Fourth, we finish. The rational approximation \(R_0\) has finitely many poles \(\zeta_1,\ldots,\zeta_N\), each lying in some component of \(\widehat{\mathbb C}\setminus K\). For each pole, use the pole-moving argument to approximate its simple kernel uniformly on \(K\) by rational functions with poles only at the corresponding chosen point of \(A\). Since there are only finitely many poles and finitely many coefficients, choose the individual approximations accurate enough that the total additional error is less than \(\varepsilon-\delta\). Taking \(\delta<\varepsilon/2\), we obtain a rational function \(r\) with poles only in \(A\) such that

\[
\sup_{z\in K}|f(z)-r(z)|<\varepsilon.
\]

If \(\mathbb C\setminus K\) is connected, the only component of \(\widehat{\mathbb C}\setminus K\) containing all finite poles can be represented by \(\infty\), so the approximants may be chosen to have their only possible pole at \(\infty\). These are exactly polynomials. This proves the polynomial approximation corollary.
